frameworks.name: finite computational precision
frameworks.one_line: Internal representations of value and probability corrupted by irreducible noise

frameworks.name: expected utility theory
frameworks.one_line: Utility and probability weighting functions derived as noise-optimal distortions

frameworks.name: reward-maximising rational choice
frameworks.one_line: Apparent irrationality reframed as optimal adaptation to noisy precision

core_question: Why do humans distort value and probability when making economic choices, given selection for reward maximisation?
key_assumptions: Decisions are irreducibly corrupted by internal noise
Distortion shape can be optimised to maximise reward and minimise uncertainty
manipulations: Factors that change the reward-maximising form of distortion
Two empirical studies probing adaptive distortion shape
key_findings: Human distortions of value and probability are approximately optimal under finite precision
Humans adapt the form of distortion when reward-maximising shape is manipulated
Apparent failures to maximise reward and preference reversals emerge from this optimal coding
interpretations: Distorted economic choices are optimal adaptations to noisy finite-precision computation rather than irrationality
Provides a normative account of why utility and probability weighting deviate from true values
